title: Improving Monte Carlo Tree Search for Symbolic Regression
abstract: Symbolic regression aims to discover concise, interpretable mathematical expressions that satisfy desired objectives, such as fitting data, posing a highly combinatorial optimization problem. While genetic programming has been the dominant approach, recent efforts have explored reinforcement learning methods for improving search efficiency. Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS), with its ability to balance exploration and exploitation through guided search, has emerged as a promising technique for symbolic expression discovery. However, its traditional bandit strategies and sequential symbol construction often limit performance. In this work, we propose an improved MCTS framework for symbolic regression that addresses these limitations through two key innovations: (1) an extreme bandit allocation strategy tailored for identifying globally optimal expressions, with finite-time performance guarantees under polynomial reward decay assumptions; and (2) evolution-inspired state-jumping actions such as mutation and crossover, which enable non-local transitions to promising regions of the search space. These state-jumping actions also reshape the reward landscape during the search process, improving both robustness and efficiency. We conduct a thorough numerical study to the impact of these improvements and benchmark our approach against existing symbolic regression methods on a variety of datasets, including both ground-truth and black-box datasets. Our approach achieves competitive performance with state-of-the-art libraries in terms of recovery rate, attains favorable positions on the Pareto frontier of accuracy versus model complexity.

reviews.comments:
SUMMARY:
This paper proposes an improved Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) framework for symbolic regression. To address the challenge of efficiently finding the global optimal symbolic expression, the framework introduces two key innovations: the first is the extreme bandit allocation strategy, which models the node selection process as a K-arm extreme bandit problem, uses a specific formula to balance exploration and exploitation, and theoretically guarantees convergence to the optimal solution in a finite time by analyzing the upper and lower bounds of the performance gap and regret value. The second is the state jump action inspired by evolution, which introduces genetic operations such as mutation and crossover, and combines bidirectional propagation to achieve non-local jumps in the search process, reshape the reward distribution, and improve the efficiency of exploring high-reward areas. The framework is based on Markov decision process (MDP) modeling and is verified by experiments on benchmark datasets such as Nguyen and Livermore.

STRENGTHS AND WEAKNESSES:
**Strengths:**

- An extreme bandit allocation strategy suitable for extreme value optimization is proposed, and the state jump mechanism in the evolutionary algorithm is organically integrated into the MCTS framework, which significantly improves the search efficiency and the ability to jump out of the local optimum.

- The upper and lower bounds of the performance of the strategy under the polynomial tail distribution are systematically derived, providing solid theoretical support for the method and filling the theoretical gap of the existing MCTS in symbolic regression.

**Weaknesses:**

- See Questions and Limitations.

QUESTIONS:
- Is the extreme bandit allocation strategy similar to the following paper? In this paper, the regret upper bounds under stationary distribution and non-stationary distribution are considered respectively. What is the difference between you two?

    Such as, [1] Hu Y Q, Liu X H, Li S Q, et al. Cascaded algorithm selection with extreme-region UCB bandit[J]. IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence, 2021, 44(10): 6782-6794.

- The derivation premise of the article requires that the reward distribution has a known polynomial tail decay characteristic. This assumption is very strong. Will it lead to situations where it is not applicable in some scenarios?

- In Appendix Table 2, it can be seen that the experimental results are extremely sensitive to hyperparameters and have poor robustness. The performance is highly dependent on the choice of two parameters. The recovery rate under different combinations varies significantly, and some combinations are almost ineffective. This shows that vanilla MCTS lacks generalization ability. In practical applications, it is difficult to find a set of universal parameters that adapt to all tasks. So is the purpose of this experiment to find the best parameter experience? Or is there another purpose? Are the parameter settings in model F used in subsequent experiments?

- The state jumping mechanism introduced by this method significantly changes the tail shape of the reward distribution, thereby improving the search efficiency. However, the change of the reward landscape is static during the execution of the algorithm. Is it possible to design a dynamic and adaptive state jumping strategy that automatically adjusts the jump probability or operation type according to the current search stage, thereby further improving the convergence and generalization ability of the search?

LIMITATIONS:
- The extreme bandit strategy relies on empirical parameters. Is it possible to introduce adaptive tuning or reward-tail-aware automatic calibration mechanism to reduce parameter dependence?

- All performance bounds and regret derivations are based on the assumption that rewards follow a specific form of beta-like distribution with a polynomial decay in the tail. I would like to know if this makes sense and why.
reviews.TITLE: Official Review

reviews.comments:
SUMMARY:
This study proposes a new Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS)-based method for symbolic regression.
Particularly, two technical contributions are made: i) an extreme bandit allocation strategy for identifying globally optimal expressions with a theoretical guarantee, and ii) state-jumping actions embedded in MCTS. While standard MCTS pursues maximizing expected cumulative rewards, this work adapts MCTS to the case of highest reward maximization. Numerical experiments support the validity of the proposed approach and also demonstrate that the proposed method achieves competitive scores compared with state-of-the-art methods.

STRENGTHS AND WEAKNESSES:
**Strengths**
- A new MCTS method that adapts to the extreme bandit case is proposed. The theoretical analysis provides an upper bound on the regret with an assumption on reward density.
- Inspired by genetic programming, state-jumping actions are introduced to the MCTS framework. Unlike prior methods that, for example, alternate between genetic programming and reinforcement learning, the proposed method rigorously embeds mutations into MCTS.
- The experiments analyze the validity of the assumption on reward density. The impact of hyperparameters is also extensively examined. Finally, in symbolic regression performance, the proposed method outperforms all baselines on Basic Benchmarks, while it also performs competitively with state-of-the-art methods on the SRBench benchmark.

---

**Weaknesses**

I feel that this work makes solid contributions from both theoretical and practical points of view, although I'm not an expert in this field. I suggest the following points to improve this work further.

**1. Discussion on the assumption.**
Theorems 1/3 present upper/lower bounds on the regret assuming the distribution of rewards has polynomial/exponential tail. While I appreciate these positive and negative claims, it would be better if the authors could elaborate more on these assumptions. Is the polynomial-tail assumption intuitively reasonable or just made artificially? Figure 2 provides empirical justification, but further analysis may give insights. Is it possible to create an artificial dataset or task for which we have better control over reward distribution to further analyze the derived bounds?

**2. Ablation.**
There are two proposed components, one about MCTS and another about mutations. However, it is unclear from the experiments how much performance gain was obtained from each in the final performance.

**3. Minor comments**
- [l.255] It was initially unclear what range [4, 6] refers to. It would be better to clarify that this is the range of the first argument of the Beta distribution.

QUESTIONS:
The questions come from the weaknesses except for the last one.
1. Is the polynomial-tail assumption intuitively reasonable or just made artificially?
2. Is it possible to create an artificial dataset or task for which we have better control over reward distribution to further analyze the derived bounds?
3. Does each of the new MCTS and mutation contribute sufficiently to the symbolic regression performance?
4. In Theorem 1, it is assumed that the first arm is optimal. Can this assumption be made without loss of generality? If not, how does this assumption become reasonable?

LIMITATIONS:
Yes.
reviews.TITLE: Official Review

reviews.comments:
SUMMARY:
This paper introduces new variants of Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) for the symbolic regression (SR) problem. Compared to the standard approach, the authors propose (1) an extreme-bandit allocation strategy and provide theoretical optimality guarantees under certain assumptions, and (2) evolution-inspired state-jumping actions. The proposed methods are evaluated against state-of-the-art baselines on common benchmarks.

STRENGTHS AND WEAKNESSES:
Strengths
-----------
* The proposed approaches are well-motivated and address relevant challenges in SR.
* The experimental results are strong and demonstrate the effectiveness of the methods.
* The heuristic designs for mutation and crossover operations appear reasonable and practically sound.

Weaknesses
----------------
Some core components of the proposed SR methods are not clearly explained. In more detail,

* For the extreme-bandit strategy, the authors state that the objective is to maximize J(T), as the gap between the maximum possible reward and the expected highest reward, and hence the regret. It is unclear why the goal is to maximize rather than minimize J(T), and the paper does not provide sufficient justification for this design choice.

* There is little information about the exact actions (i.e., which symbols) used when modeling the MDP. How are numerical expressions in SR generated? Are they also produced via MCTS? The paper provides limited details about these aspects of the SR formulation, even in the appendix.

QUESTIONS:
Please see the weaknesses section above.

LIMITATIONS:
Some core components of the proposed SR methods are not clearly explained. The paper is good, but the issues raised above need to be addressed for the paper to be acceptable.
reviews.TITLE: Official Review

reviews.comments:
DECISION:
Accept (poster)

COMMENT:
This paper introduces new variants of Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) for the symbolic regression (SR) problem. Compared to the standard approach, the authors propose (1) an extreme-bandit allocation strategy and provide theoretical optimality guarantees under certain assumptions, and (2) evolution-inspired state-jumping actions. The proposed methods are evaluated against state-of-the-art baselines on common benchmarks.

The paper has made significant contributions in (1) The proposed approaches are well-motivated and address relevant challenges in SR. In particular, an extreme bandit allocation strategy suitable for extreme value optimization is integrated into SR. (2) The experimental results are strong and demonstrate the effectiveness of the methods. (3) The heuristic designs for mutation and crossover operations appear reasonable and practically sound.

At the same time, the reviewers raise questions regarding the experimental comparisons and the ablation study. The most important aspect is whether the conditions used in theoretical analysis are widely applicable in a real-world context. The authors are encouraged to take into account the reviewers' feedback.
reviews.TITLE: Decision
